title: Ethanol Blended Petrol (EBP) Programme and Ethanol Properties
focus: India's ethanol blending targets, properties of ethanol, feedstocks allowed under the National Policy on Biofuels, and government initiatives promoting blending.
rationale: The EBP programme is a flagship initiative with updated targets (20% by 2025-26). Ethanol's chemical properties, by-products (DDGS), and India's global standing are highly relevant for Prelims.
sectionExcerpt: The EBP Programme, launched in 2003, aims to mix ethanol with petrol for cleaner fuel. The National Policy on Biofuels advanced the 20% blending target (E20) to 2025-26. Ethanol is a renewable, high-octane fuel produced via fermentation of sugars or petrochemical processes, but is 27% less energy-dense than petrol. India is the world's third-largest producer and consumer of ethanol. Initiatives include PM JIVAN Yojana for 2G ethanol, Ethanol Interest Subvention Schemes, and GST reduction to 5%.
evidenceBullets: Target of 20% ethanol blending in petrol was advanced to 2025-26 from 2030 under the National Policy on Biofuels.
Ethanol is 27% less energy-dense than petrol but has a high-octane rating.
India is the world's third-largest producer and consumer of ethanol, after the USA and Brazil.
Distillers' dried grains (DDGS) are a by-product of grain ethanol reused as animal feed.
PM JIVAN Yojana facilitates Second Generation (2G) ethanol projects.
GST on ethanol meant for the EBP Programme was reduced from 18% to 5%.

title: Generations of Biofuels: 1G to 4G
focus: Classification of biofuels into first, second, third, and fourth generations based on their feedstocks.
rationale: UPSC frequently asks about the classification of biofuels and the specific feedstocks that fall under 1G, 2G, and 3G categories.
sectionExcerpt: Biofuels are categorized into four generations based on their feedstock. 1st Generation uses edible feedstocks like wheat, corn, sugarcane, and food-grade oils. 2nd Generation utilizes lignocellulosic biomass and waste, such as wheat bran, animal fats, used cooking oil, and Jatropha. 3rd Generation is derived from microalgae and cyanobacteria biomass. 4th Generation involves genetically engineered microorganisms like Escherichia coli and Saccharomyces cerevisiae.
evidenceBullets: 1st Generation biofuels use edible feedstocks like wheat, corn, sugarcane, and palm oil.
2nd Generation biofuels use non-edible lignocellulosic biomass and waste, including Jatropha curcas and used cooking oil.
3rd Generation biofuels are produced from microalgae and cyanobacteria biomass.
4th Generation biofuels utilize genetically engineered microorganisms such as Escherichia coli.

title: Alternative Biofuels: Bioethanol, E-Methanol, and Compressed Biogas (CBG)
focus: Recent developments, production processes, and applications of Bioethanol (bamboo-based), E-Methanol, and CBG.
rationale: Emerging alternative fuels like E-Methanol and CBG are critical for India's energy transition. The specific composition of CBG and the production process of E-Methanol are prime targets for science and technology questions.
sectionExcerpt: India's first Bamboo-based Bio-Ethanol Plant was inaugurated in Assam; bamboo is a 2G ethanol feedstock due to high lignocellulose. The world's first commercial E-Methanol plant opened in Denmark. E-Methanol is produced by combining green hydrogen (from water electrolysis) with captured CO2. NITI Aayog's 'Methanol Economy' Programme promotes its use. India's First Cooperative Multi-Feed CBG Plant opened in Maharashtra. CBG is prepared from raw biogas (anaerobic digestion of biomass), which mainly contains 55-60% Methane and 35-40% Carbon dioxide.
evidenceBullets: Bamboo is an attractive 2G ethanol feedstock due to its high lignocellulose content and non-food nature.
E-Methanol is a low-carbon fuel produced by combining green hydrogen with captured carbon dioxide.
NITI Aayog spearheads the 'Methanol Economy' Programme in India, promoting blending of DME (Di-methyl Ether) in LPG.
Raw biogas is produced by anaerobic digestion of biomass and waste sources.
Biogas primarily consists of Methane (55-60%) and Carbon dioxide (35-40%), along with impurities like H2S.
